title_en: Tahdhib at-Tahdhib: Ibn Hajar's Biographical Dictionary
title_ar: تهذيب التهذيب: قاموس ابن حجر في تراجم الرواة
content_en:
Tahdhib at-Tahdhib ('The Refinement of the Refinement') is Ibn Hajar al-Asqalani's comprehensive biographical dictionary of hadith narrators — a monumental work that refined and extended the earlier Tahdhib al-Kamal of al-Mizzi. It covers the narrators whose names appear in the six canonical hadith collections (the kutub as-sittah: al-Bukhari, Muslim, Abu Dawud, at-Tirmidhi, an-Nasa'i, and Ibn Majah), along with narrators from several supplementary collections. In its standard printed edition it runs to approximately twelve to fourteen volumes and is one of the most important reference works in the entire hadith sciences tradition.

Ibn Hajar's purpose in producing Tahdhib at-Tahdhib was not to replace al-Mizzi's Tahdhib al-Kamal but to make it more practically useful. Al-Mizzi's work was enormously valuable but extremely detailed — its entries often ran to extensive lengths with full citation of every evaluation by every critic who had addressed the narrator in question. This comprehensiveness was valuable for specialist research but made the work unwieldy for ordinary reference use. Ibn Hajar condensed al-Mizzi's entries while retaining their essential information and adding later scholarship that al-Mizzi had not been able to include.

The organization of Tahdhib at-Tahdhib follows the alphabetical arrangement standard for biographical dictionaries in the Arabic tradition: entries are ordered by the first letter of the narrator's personal name (ism), with names beginning with the same letter further organized by the second letter. This alphabetical arrangement made the dictionary practical as a reference tool — a scholar needing information about a specific narrator could locate the entry directly rather than searching through chronologically or thematically organized material.

Ibn Hajar later produced an even more concise version called Taqrib at-Tahdhib ('The Facilitation of the Refinement'), which reduced each entry to a single sentence giving the narrator's name, their grade of reliability, the generation they belonged to, and the year of their death. The Taqrib became even more widely used for quick reference than the Tahdhib, because scholars who needed a rapid assessment of a narrator's reliability could find it in an instant rather than working through a multi-page entry. Both works remain indispensable tools for hadith scholarship.

Tahdhib at-Tahdhib reflects Ibn Hajar's encyclopedic knowledge of the biographical tradition. He had read and absorbed essentially all of the major narrator criticism literature — the works of Yahya ibn Ma'in, Ahmad ibn Hanbal, al-Bukhari, Ibn Abi Hatim, an-Nasa'i, and dozens of others — and synthesized their evaluations in each entry, indicating where critics agreed and where they disagreed and providing his own assessment based on the available evidence.
